User Story: As a developer, when I import apibase in a configured Django project, I want graphene-django form-field converters to be registered automatically so that list filters expose correct GraphQL types.
Expected Behavior: If Django settings are configured at import time, importing apibase triggers import of apibase.graphql, which registers converters for ListCharField, ListIntegerField, MonthRangeField, and MultipleChoiceField. If settings are not yet configured, registration is deferred until apibase.graphql is explicitly imported.

User Story: As a maintainer, I want the package to expose a version string so that consumers and tests can assert compatibility.
Expected Behavior: apibase.__version__ returns the current package version. Note: apibase/__init__.py currently reads '0.4.0' while pyproject.toml reads '0.4.1'.
Notes: Version mismatch between source and package metadata should be aligned.

User Story: As a developer, I want to override apibase behavior via a Django settings dict so that URLs, URNs, and storage paths match my deployment.
Expected Behavior: apibase_settings reads the APIBASE dict from Django settings. It provides lazy attribute access, caching, and import-string resolution. Unknown attributes raise AttributeError.

User Story: As a developer, I want to configure the URN namespace identifier used across the app.
Expected Behavior: Default is 'x-nid'; overridden by APIBASE['URN_NID']. Used by model_urn and parse_urn.

User Story: As a developer, I want to configure the URN namespace-specific string.
Expected Behavior: Default is 'self'. Used by model_urn and rest_endpoint_from_urn to build or parse URNs.

User Story: As a developer, I want REST endpoint URLs generated by the library to use my deployment domain and scheme.
Expected Behavior: DOMAIN defaults to None (falls back to django.contrib.sites), SCHEME defaults to 'https'. HOST defaults to 'self' but is currently declared and never read by any code path.
Notes: HOST setting is dead configuration; consider removing or implementing intended behavior.

User Story: As a developer, I want to control the URL path prefix used for storage-based file downloads.
Expected Behavior: Default is 'storage'. Used by DownloadMixin.download_filefield_storage and LocalPathResolver.construct_filename.

User Story: As an API consumer, I want every model instance to be addressable by a stable URN so that I can reference resources across services.
Expected Behavior: identity.urn(instance, nss=None, nid=None) delegates to model_urn using APIBASE defaults, producing 'urn:{nid}:{nss}:{app_label}:{model_name}:{pk}'.

User Story: As an API consumer, I want the canonical REST detail URL for any model instance.
Expected Behavior: identity.endpoint(instance, url_name=None, pk_name='pk') uses instance.get_endpoint_url() when present; otherwise reverses 'api-{app_label}-{model_name}-detail'. Soft-fails to empty string on any exception so serialization does not crash.

User Story: As an API consumer, I want a human-readable string for any resource.
Expected Behavior: identity.display(instance) returns str(instance).

User Story: As an API consumer, I want relative paths turned into absolute URLs when a request/builder is available.
Expected Behavior: identity.absolute_uri(path, *, builder, fallback) returns builder.build_absolute_uri(path) when builder is not None; otherwise returns fallback. Used by REST EndpointField and GraphQL NodeMixin.resolve_endpoint.

User Story: As a developer, I want thin serializer-side aliases for identity helpers.
Expected Behavior: to_urn, endpoint_from_urn, and drf_endpoint are thin wrappers around identity.urn, rest_endpoint_from_urn, and identity.endpoint respectively.

User Story: As an API consumer, I want every model instance to be addressable by a stable URN so that I can reference resources across services.
Expected Behavior: model_urn(instance) returns 'urn:{nid}:{nss}:{app_label}:{model_name}:{pk}'. If the instance lacks _meta, returns empty string. Settings URN_NID and URN_NSS can be overridden per call.

User Story: As a developer, I want to parse a URN and verify its namespace before resolving it.
Expected Behavior: parse_urn splits a colon-separated URN into nid, nss, app_label, model_name, and others. Returns None if the parsed nid does not match the expected nid. NOTE: the prefix argument is shadowed by a tuple unpack, so prefix validation is currently a no-op.
Notes: Latent bug FIXED 2026-06-24: prefix is now validated (was self-compared/shadowed). Regression tests in tests/test_urn.py.

User Story: As an API consumer, I want to convert a URN back into a REST URL so that I can navigate from a GraphQL/URN reference to a DRF endpoint.
Expected Behavior: Builds '{scheme}://{service}{domain}{prefix}/{app_label}/{model_name}/{others...}'. Domain resolution order: explicit argument -> APIBASE['DOMAIN'] -> current site domain (last two labels). Returns None on parse failure. nss='self' omits the service subdomain. HOST setting is not used.

User Story: As a developer, I want a DRF permission class that ties view access to a Django permission code so that I can reuse Django auth for API security.
Expected Behavior: Permission subclasses IsAuthenticated. Subclasses define PERM_CODE and optionally PRIVATE. has_permission returns True if user is authenticated and (PRIVATE is False and method is safe) or user has the permission. Logs denials.

User Story: As a developer, I want a helper that tells me whether the current request is read-only.
Expected Behavior: is_safe_method(request) returns True when request.method is in DRF SAFE_METHODS (GET, HEAD, OPTIONS).

User Story: As a GraphQL developer, I want to guard resolver methods with a permission check so that unauthorized users receive null instead of data.
Expected Behavior: Permission.has(func) is a class-method decorator that wraps a resolver. If check_info fails, returns None; otherwise calls the resolver.

User Story: As a GraphQL developer, I want to guard a resolver with an arbitrary Django permission string.
Expected Behavior: has_perms(func, permission) wraps a resolver and returns None if info.context.user lacks the permission.

User Story: As a GraphQL developer, I want to decide whether to return a full queryset based on privacy settings and user privileges.
Expected Behavior: Permission.has_query_permission returns True if PRIVATE is False and the request method is safe (method='GET'), or if the user has the permission. is_staff is not a bypass; superusers are granted only via Django's has_perm.

User Story: As a developer, I want a single function to decide whether a user can perform an action so that REST and GraphQL permissions behave consistently.
Expected Behavior: can(user, perm, *, method=None, private=True) returns False when user is None. If private is False and method is a safe method, returns True. Otherwise delegates to user.has_perm(perm).

User Story: As an API consumer, I want each serialized resource to include its own REST detail URL so that I can navigate the API.
Expected Behavior: EndpointField is read-only with source='*'. It calls identity.endpoint(instance), optionally following attr_name for related objects, and builds an absolute URL when a request is in the serializer context. Empty endpoint path is mapped to None.

User Story: As an API consumer, I want each serialized resource to include its URN so that I can use cross-service identifiers.
Expected Behavior: UrnField is read-only with source='*'. Returns identity.urn(instance) or, via attr_name, the URN of a related object.

User Story: As an API consumer, I want a human-readable string for each resource and related resource.
Expected Behavior: DisplayField is read-only with source='*'. Returns identity.display(instance) or str(related_object) when attr_name is set.

User Story: As a developer, I want a ModelSerializer that automatically adds id, endpoint, urn, and display fields.
Expected Behavior: BaseModelSerializer declares id as IntegerField(required=False), endpoint as EndpointField, urn as UrnField, display as DisplayField. These are available unless explicitly excluded.

User Story: As an API consumer, I want to create or update a parent and its children in a single request so that I can reduce API round trips.
Expected Behavior: BaseModelSerializer.nested_fields lists child field names. During create/update, scalar fields are saved first, then each nested field's child serializer receives the parent FK (or generic-relation defaults) and calls update_or_create for each child dict. Supports reverse FK and GenericRelation; OneToOneRel is wrapped in a single-item list.
Notes: Tests currently fail in this environment due to missing tests_parent table (pytest-django harness issue).

User Story: As an API consumer, when I update a parent I want unlisted children to be deleted so that the child list exactly matches my payload.
Expected Behavior: NestedOrphanDeleteMixin, placed before BaseModelSerializer, deletes existing children not present in the saved result when the field is listed in nested_fields_orphan_delete and was explicitly present in the payload. Field absence preserves existing children. OneToOneRel raises NotImplementedError.
Notes: Tests currently fail in this environment due to missing tests_parent table (pytest-django harness issue).

User Story: As a developer, I want to dispatch a Django signal after nested children are processed so that other code can react to the change.
Expected Behavior: BaseModelSerializer.nested_fields_updateds_signal, if set, is sent with sender=model and instance=instance after all nested fields are processed.
Notes: Untested and undocumented; high regression risk.

User Story: As a developer, I want to run action-specific validation and save hooks based on the current ViewSet action.
Expected Behavior: BaseModelSerializer.action_handlers maps action names (or '*' wildcard) to Action subclasses. On __init__, handlers are instantiated. is_valid calls _validate_for_action; save calls _save_for_action. If no handler matches, save forwards **kwargs to DRF's normal save.

User Story: As a developer, I want to create or update a model instance directly from a serializer class.
Expected Behavior: BaseModelSerializer.update_or_create fetches by id, instantiates the serializer with optional partial/context, validates, saves, and returns the instance.

User Story: As an API consumer submitting form-encoded or multipart data, I want nested child lists to be handled correctly.
Expected Behavior: BaseModelSerializer.run_validation detects QueryDict input and copies nested_field keys into _children_set while passing the rest to normal validation.

User Story: As a developer, I want convenient access to the current view action and request user inside serializer methods.
Expected Behavior: BaseModelSerializer.view_action returns context.view.action; request_user returns context.request.user.

User Story: As a developer, I want to mutate the serialized output for special cases without overriding to_representation entirely.
Expected Behavior: If the serializer defines patch_result(instance, data), BaseModelSerializer.to_representation calls it with the serialized dict and uses the result.

User Story: As a developer, I want a legacy alias serializer that explicitly redeclares the URN field.
Expected Behavior: UrnModelSerializer subclasses BaseModelSerializer and redeclares urn = UrnField(). Functionally equivalent to BaseModelSerializer.

User Story: As a developer, I want batch update serializers to preserve the lookup id during internal value conversion.
Expected Behavior: BatchSerializerMixin.to_internal_value preserves the id attribute (or Meta.update_lookup_field) when the serializer is a child of BatchListSerializer and the request method is PUT/PATCH.

User Story: As an API consumer, I want to update many records in one PATCH request keyed by id.
Expected Behavior: BatchListSerializer.update maps incoming data by update_lookup_field (default 'id'), validates that all IDs exist in the queryset, then calls child.update for each matched instance. Raises ValidationError if IDs are missing or invalid.

User Story: As a developer, I want a small reusable object to implement per-action validation, save, delete, and signal dispatch.
Expected Behavior: Action receives the serializer in __init__. save(saving) calls saving.save() then dispatch(). delete(deleting) calls deleting.delete(). validate() is a no-op by default. dispatch() sends the configured signal with sender, instance, action, and extra fields.

User Story: As an API consumer, I want to filter by primary key using common predicates without declaring them manually.
Expected Behavior: BaseFilter provides pk alias, id__includes, id__excludes, id__in_csv, and id__not_in_csv on top of standard django-filter behavior.

User Story: As an API consumer, I want choice filters backed by integer fields to coerce values to integers.
Expected Behavior: BaseFilter.filter_for_lookup swaps auto-generated ChoiceFilters for IntFilter when lookup_type='exact' and the underlying field is an IntegerField. IntFilter uses forms.IntegerField internally.

User Story: As an API consumer searching in Japanese, I want keywords to match regardless of full-width/half-width differences.
Expected Behavior: WordFilter splits input on delimiters (default '[\s\u3000,]+'), then for each token builds an OR query across lookups using both zen2han and han2zen forms via jaconv, ANDing tokens. Empty value returns queryset unchanged. Honors distinct.

User Story: As an API consumer, I want to filter by a comma-separated list of strings using the '__in' predicate.
Expected Behavior: ListCharInFilter accepts a list of strings and applies field_name__in. Backed by ListCharField with SelectMultiple widget.

User Story: As an API consumer, I want to filter by a comma-separated list of integers.
Expected Behavior: ListIntegerInFilter converts input values to integers and applies field_name__in. Backed by ListIntegerField.

User Story: As an API consumer, I want to filter strings within an inclusive range.
Expected Behavior: CharRangeFilter applies gte/lte lookups. Skips filtering when both start and stop are empty. Backed by CharRangeField/CharRangeWidget with suffixes 'after'/'before'.

User Story: As an API consumer, I want to filter records by YYYYMM month ranges.
Expected Behavior: MonthFromToRangeFilter compresses ['YYYYMM','YYYYMM'] into a slice spanning the start of the first month to the last day of the second month. Backed by MonthRangeField.

User Story: As an API consumer, I want a multiple-choice filter populated from distinct values already present in the database.
Expected Behavior: AllValuesMultipleFilter builds choices from the model's distinct values for the field and filters with field_name__in.

User Story: As a developer, I want to reuse an existing FilterSet's fields under a related-name prefix.
Expected Behavior: clone_filter_fields clones declared and base filters of a filterset, prefixing field_name and lookups (for WordFilter). Useful for building cross-model filters.

User Story: As a developer, I want to dynamically compose a new FilterSet from multiple related FilterSets.
Expected Behavior: make_related_filterset(type_name, distinct=True, **related_filters) creates a new FilterSet subclass by cloning each related filterset with its related-name prefix.

User Story: As a developer, I want a FilterSet to be able to create a prefixed clone of itself for a related path.
Expected Behavior: RelatedFilterSetMixin.adds create_related_filterset(cls, related_name) that returns a cloned filterset with field names prefixed by related_name__.

User Story: As a developer, I want a form field that normalizes comma-separated or multiple string input into a list.
Expected Behavior: ListCharField validates that input is list/tuple, converts each item with str, and returns [] for empty input. Uses SelectMultiple widget.

User Story: As a developer, I want a form field that normalizes multiple integer input into a list of ints.
Expected Behavior: ListIntegerField validates list/tuple input and converts each item with int. Empty input returns []. Uses SelectMultiple widget.

User Story: As a developer, I want a form field for string ranges with after/before suffixes.
Expected Behavior: CharRangeField is composed of two CharFields. CharRangeWidget normalizes list-wrapped values and uses suffixes 'after'/'before'.

User Story: As a developer, I want a form field that turns YYYYMM start/end into a month slice.
Expected Behavior: MonthRangeField extends CharRangeField; compress() returns slice(start_of_month, end_of_last_day_of_month). Registered as String in GraphQL converters.

User Story: As an API consumer, I want a single ViewSet to support standard DRF CRUD plus batch create/update and file download.
Expected Behavior: BaseModelViewSet combines ModelViewSet, ViewSetMixin, and DownloadMixin. Uses Pagination by default. Provides batch_create and batch_update actions.

User Story: As an API consumer, I want to create multiple records with one POST to a batch endpoint.
Expected Behavior: POST /{basename}/batch_create/ accepts a list, validates with many=True, calls perform_create, and returns 201 CREATED with serialized results and success headers. GET falls back to list.

User Story: As an API consumer, I want to update multiple records with one PATCH to a batch endpoint.
Expected Behavior: PATCH /{basename}/batch_update/ accepts a list keyed by id, validates against the filtered queryset with many=True and partial=True, calls perform_update, and returns serialized results. GET falls back to list. Queryset filtering (e.g. ?category=A) limits the update scope.

User Story: As an API consumer, I want to download a file stored in a model's FileField by record id and field name.
Expected Behavior: GET /{basename}/{pk}/{field}/download/ calls get_object(), resolves the file attribute, and serves it with Content-Disposition. Customizable via get_download_filefield_name and create_download_filefield_response.

User Story: As an API consumer, I want to download a file given its storage path without knowing the record id.
Expected Behavior: GET /{basename}/{STORAGE_PREFIX}/{field}/{name}.{format} resolves the instance via LocalPathResolver.find(model, field, path) and serves the file with Content-Disposition.

User Story: As an API consumer, I want to export the full queryset as CSV without pagination.
Expected Behavior: When HTTP_ACCEPT is 'text/csv', BaseModelViewSet.paginate_queryset returns None. get_renderer_context forces utf-8-sig encoding, builds header from fields_query (class attribute default None; request ?fields_query=col1,col2 overrides), and labels from serializer field labels.

User Story: As a developer, I want to introspect a ViewSet to discover the Django Permission objects it requires.
Expected Behavior: ViewSetMixin.permissions() iterates permission_classes, filters for apibase.permissions.Permission subclasses with a PERM_CODE, and returns matching Django Permission queryset results (first per class).

User Story: As a developer, I want to branch logic inside a ViewSet based on whether the request is safe.
Expected Behavior: is_safe_method property returns permissions.is_safe_method(self.request).

User Story: As a developer, I want a thin wrapper around Django's static serve that optionally adds Content-Disposition.
Expected Behavior: static_serve calls django.views.static.serve and, if name is given, sets Content-Disposition using utils.to_content_disposition. document_root defaults to '/'.

User Story: As a developer, I want a DRF router that produces predictable 'api-{app_label}-{model_name}' basenames.
Expected Behavior: DefaultRouter.get_default_basename returns 'api-{app_label}-{model_name}' when queryset is present; otherwise falls back to module/viewset name with 'viewset' stripped. Also accepts an optional root_view_name kwarg.

User Story: As an API consumer, I want paginated list responses that include count, page range, current page, next/previous, and results.
Expected Behavior: Pagination subclasses PageNumberPagination with page_size=16, max_page_size=200, page_size_query_param='page_size'. Response includes count, page_range, current_page, next, previous, results.

User Story: As an API consumer, I want to request resources in CSV, TSV, PDF, XLSX, or ZIP formats.
Expected Behavior: CsvRenderer subclasses rest_framework_csv.CSVRenderer. BinaryRenderer returns data unchanged; PdfRenderer, XlsxRenderer, ZipballRenderer subclass it for their media types. TsvRenderer handles text/tab-separated-values. BrowsableAPIRendererWithoutForms suppresses HTML forms.

User Story: As a developer, I want a convenient tuple of all apibase renderers to add to DRF settings.
Expected Behavior: RENDERERS combines api_settings.DEFAULT_RENDERER_CLASSES with the apibase custom renderers.

User Story: As a developer, I want to reverse a DRF action URL for a model instance or class.
Expected Behavior: endpoint(instance_or_model, action, **params) reverses 'api-{app_label}-{model_name}-{action}' (underscores in action become dashes). Adds pk kwarg automatically when the instance has a truthy id.

User Story: As a developer, I want to turn a relative path into an absolute URL using the current request or site.
Expected Behavior: absolute_path(path, request=None) builds an absolute URL from request when provided; otherwise uses django.contrib.sites Site.objects.get_current() and https.

User Story: As a developer, I want a single call that reverses a model action URL and makes it absolute.
Expected Behavior: endpoint_url combines endpoint() and absolute_path().

User Story: As a developer writing tests or scripts, I want to build a WSGIRequest with a user and query params.
Expected Behavior: create_request(url='/', user=None, params=None, site=None) parses scheme/host/port/path/query, defaults to AnonymousUser, merges params into environ, and falls back to example.com if sites framework is unavailable.

User Story: As a developer, I want deterministic storage paths based on date, ULID, content type, field name, and access prefix.
Expected Behavior: LocalPathResolver.__call__(instance, filename) returns construct_filename(..., create_path(filename, instance)). create_path builds 'YYYY-MM-DD/<ulid>.<ext>'. resolve_content_type uses ContentType.objects.get_for_model(instance) (or instance.content_type when RESOLVE_CONTNT_TYPE is True).

User Story: As a developer, I want to find a model instance from a stored file path.
Expected Behavior: LocalPathResolver.find(model_class, field_name, path) looks up the field's upload-to resolver and calls find_instance, which finds the first instance whose constructed filename matches the stored file path.

User Story: As a developer, I want to build ZIP archives in memory and output them to streams, files, or Django ContentFile.
Expected Behavior: Zipball.append adds files with ZIP_DEFLATED and create_system=0. read() returns bytes; write_to(stream) writes to a file-like object; write_to_file saves to disk; to_contentfile returns ContentFile.

User Story: As a developer, I want to serialize querysets to JSON files inside a ZIP archive.
Expected Behavior: ModelZipball.append_query(queryset) serializes the queryset and stores it as '{app_label}.{model_name}.json' in the archive.

User Story: As a developer, I want a GraphQL view that uses DRF authentication and permissions.
Expected Behavior: DRFAuthenticatedGraphQLView wraps graphene_django.views.GraphQLView with DRF IsAuthenticated, default auth classes, and api_view(['GET','POST']). parse_body returns request.data for DRF Requests; otherwise falls back to Graphene parsing.

User Story: As a developer, I want an authenticated endpoint that returns the GraphQL schema as SDL.
Expected Behavior: sdl(request) returns the GraphQL schema printed as SDL with text/plain content type. Requires DRF authentication via _decorate.

User Story: As a downstream developer with legacy imports, I want the old DRFGraphQLView name to keep working.
Expected Behavior: DRFGraphQLView is an alias for DRFAuthenticatedGraphQLView.

User Story: As a developer, I want apibase modules to import and use drf-spectacular decorators even when the package is not installed, so that the library has an optional OpenAPI dependency.
Expected Behavior: When drf_spectacular is installed, re-exports extend_schema, extend_schema_field, OpenApiParameter, OpenApiTypes. When absent, provides no-op decorators and stub classes (OpenApiTypes.URI/STR = None, OpenApiParameter.PATH = 'path').

User Story: As a developer, I want to post-process resolved field values on a per-type basis without writing explicit resolvers.
Expected Behavior: default_resolver wraps the stock graphene resolver. After lookup, if the parent type exposes patch_result(res, attname, default_value, root, info, **args), it returns the patched value.

User Story: As a developer, I want SerializerMutation to accept a Relay client_mutation_id and decode it into the model's primary key.
Expected Behavior: BaseSerializerMutation.get_serializer_kwargs uses from_global_id on client_mutation_id (when it is a string) and writes the decoded database id into input['id'].
Notes: Abstract Meta; concrete mutations must subclass.

User Story: As a GraphQL consumer, I want Node types to expose pk, endpoint, urn, and display fields automatically.
Expected Behavior: NodeMixin provides resolve_pk, resolve_endpoint (absolute when build_absolute_uri exists), resolve_urn, resolve_display, and get_node (fetches by pk via get_queryset).

User Story: As a GraphQL consumer, I want paginated connections to expose total_count, records count, and an optional summary payload.
Expected Behavior: SummaryMixin provides resolve_total_count (self.length), resolve_records (order_by('id').distinct().count() for querysets), and resolve_summary (calls queryset.summary() and encodes with JSONEncode).

User Story: As a developer, I want a Relay connection class that combines connection fields with summary aggregates.
Expected Behavior: FilteringConnection subclasses graphene.relay.Connection and SummaryMixin. It is dynamically named '{prefix}{Node}NodeSetConnection' by NodeSet.

User Story: As a GraphQL consumer, I want a connection field that auto-generates filter arguments from a django-filter FilterSet and applies conditional distinct().
Expected Behavior: NodeSet extends DjangoFilterConnectionField. filtering_args uses utils.get_filtering_args_from_filterset with IntFilter as obvious filter. resolve_queryset applies .distinct() only when a filter has distinct=True or traverses M2M/reverse-FK, or the queryset itself has duplicating joins. Sets has_previous_page based on start offset.

User Story: As a developer, I want the library to avoid unnecessary .distinct() calls while still removing duplicates from M2M/reverse-FK filters.
Expected Behavior: _filter_needs_distinct checks filter.distinct and relation traversal. _queryset_has_duplicating_joins inspects alias_map for M2M/reverse-FK joins and conservatively returns True on error. _needs_distinct orchestrates both and only considers filters present in args.

User Story: As a developer, I want to reuse the same node type in multiple NodeSet fields without GraphQL type-name collisions.
Expected Behavior: NodeSet accepts name_prefix kwarg; generated connection type is named '{prefix}{Type}NodeSetConnection'.

User Story: As a developer, I want to serialize GraphQL data to JSON with Decimal values as floats.
Expected Behavior: JSONEncode subclasses DjangoJSONEncoder and converts decimal.Decimal to float; all other types fall back to DjangoJSONEncoder.

User Story: As a developer or test author, I want to flatten Relay edges/node wrappers from GraphQL response data.
Expected Behavior: strip_relay recursively removes edges/node wrappers. List of edges becomes list of nodes; dicts are recursively stripped when recursive=True; scalars pass through.

User Story: As a developer, I want to expose django-filter FilterSet fields as Graphene arguments.
Expected Behavior: Maps filterset base_filters to graphene Arguments. Declared filters and filters in obvious_filters use the filter's own field; otherwise derives from the model field's formfield. RangeFilters emit suffixed args (_min/_max) when widget.suffixes exist.

User Story: As a developer, I want to compute the start offset for Relay pagination from an after cursor.
Expected Behavior: resolve_start_offset(slice_start, after) uses get_offset_with_default and returns max(slice_start - 1, after_offset, -1) + 1.

User Story: As a developer, I want convenient helpers to execute GraphQL queries in tests, scripts, or server code.
Expected Behavior: gql_query executes a query string against a schema using gql.Client. query dispatches to query_instance when instance or object_name+id are provided (encoding id as Relay global id); otherwise calls gql_query. Schema defaults to graphene_settings.SCHEMA.

User Story: As a developer, I want to build Relay global IDs from type name and primary key.
Expected Behavior: to_gql_relay_id(schema_name, id) returns graphql_relay.to_global_id(schema_name, id).

User Story: As a developer, I want RFC 6266 Content-Disposition headers that support UTF-8 filenames.
Expected Behavior: to_content_disposition(filename) returns 'attachment; filename*=utf-8''{url_quoted_filename}'.

User Story: As a developer, I want to parse a Content-Disposition header and extract the UTF-8 filename.
Expected Behavior: get_filename_from_header(header) searches for 'filename*...utf-8''...' and returns the unquoted value; returns None if missing or not UTF-8.

User Story: As a developer, I want to store GraphQL query templates per model and load them by convention.
Expected Behavior: query_model_file accepts a model class, instance, or 'app_label.model_name' string. Default template is 'query.graphql' when id/instance is present, otherwise 'query_set.graphql'. Path: '{app_label}/{model_name}/{name}.graphql'.

User Story: As a developer, I want to load a model's GraphQL template, execute it, and optionally strip Relay wrappers.
Expected Behavior: query_model loads the template, calls apibase.utils.query, and optionally applies strip_relay(recursive=True).

User Story: As a developer, I want to build a GraphQL HTTP request payload from a model template.
Expected Behavior: query_params returns {'query': template_source, 'variables': {...}}. If id is present, it is encoded as a Relay global id.

User Story: As a GraphQL consumer, I want list-of-string filters to expose [String!] arguments instead of String.
Expected Behavior: Converter registered for apibase.fields.ListCharField returns graphene.List(graphene.NonNull(graphene.String)) preserving required.

User Story: As a GraphQL consumer, I want list-of-integer filters to expose [Int!] arguments.
Expected Behavior: Converter registered for apibase.fields.ListIntegerField returns graphene.List(graphene.NonNull(graphene.Int)).

User Story: As a GraphQL consumer, I want month-range filters to expose a String argument.
Expected Behavior: Converter registered for apibase.fields.MonthRangeField returns graphene.String.

User Story: As a GraphQL consumer, I want django_filters MultipleChoiceField to expose [String!].
Expected Behavior: Converter registered for django_filters.fields.MultipleChoiceField returns graphene.List(graphene.NonNull(graphene.String)).

User Story: As a developer using apibase in a CLI or packaging context, I want import apibase to succeed even when Django settings are not configured.
Expected Behavior: apibase/__init__.py only imports apibase.graphql when settings.configured is True; otherwise converter registration is deferred. test_importing_apibase_without_configured_django_does_not_raise verifies this.

User Story: As a developer, I want to chain queryset transformations (prefetching, authorization) on a custom DjangoObjectType.
Expected Behavior: apibase.gql.types.DjangoObjectType subclasses graphene_django's DjangoObjectType. Its get_queryset calls resolve_queryset(queryset, info, resolvers=cls.queryset_resolvers), applying each resolver in order.

User Story: As a developer, I want a helper that applies a list of (queryset, info) -> (queryset, info) functions.
Expected Behavior: resolve_queryset uses functools.reduce to apply each resolver in sequence. If resolvers is empty, returns (queryset, info) unchanged.

User Story: As a developer, I want to create a WebSocket GraphQL consumer for a specific schema.
Expected Behavior: GraphqlWsConsumer extends channels_graphql_ws.GraphqlWsConsumer and logs connections in on_connect. schema_consumer_class(schema, name) dynamically creates a consumer subclass with schema attribute set. create_schema_consumer is the same alias.

User Story: As a developer, I want to serialize Django model field metadata into a migration-deconstruction representation.
Expected Behavior: ModelFieldSerializer.to_representation returns {'model': {app_label, model_name}, 'field': {name, type, args, kwargs}}. args/kwargs are serialized using Django's migration serializer.

User Story: As an admin user, I want to list or retrieve metadata for all model fields, scoped by app or model.
Expected Behavior: ModelFieldViewSet provides list(app_label=None, model_name=None) and retrieve(app_label, model_name, field_name). Requires IsAdminUser. Uses ContentType.objects.get_by_natural_key.

User Story: As a developer, I want URL patterns for the meta field API without trailing slashes.
Expected Behavior: CustomReadOnlyRouter subclasses SimpleRouter. urlpatterns exposes GET /{app_label}/{model_name}/{field_name} and list variants for all/app/model scopes.

User Story: As a developer, I want out-of-the-box GraphQL types for User, Group, Permission, and ContentType.
Expected Behavior: apibase.contrib.schema.query defines User, Group, Permission, ContentType object types mixing NodeMixin and DjangoObjectType. User exposes permissions (via all_permissions) and excludes password. convert_choices_to_enum=False.

User Story: As a developer, I want Relay node fields and NodeSet list fields for auth models.
Expected Behavior: UserQueryMixin, GroupQueryMixin, PermissionQueryMixin, ConteTypeQueryMixin add {model} (node field) and {model}_set (NodeSet). Query combines all mixins.

User Story: As a developer, I want FilterSets for User, Group, Permission, and ContentType.
Expected Behavior: UserFilter, GroupFilter, PermissionFilter, ContentTypeFilter subclass BaseFilter. They currently exclude [''] meaning all fields are exposed.

User Story: As a developer, I want a ready-to-use GraphQL schema combining auth queries.
Expected Behavior: apibase.contrib.schema defines schema = graphene.Schema(query=Query, auto_camelcase=False) and exec_query(query_string, **params) as a convenience wrapper around gql_query.

User Story: As a developer, I want to export groups with their permissions to JSON and re-import them later.
Expected Behavior: export_groups runs a hard-coded GraphQL query and returns JSON. import_groups(path) reads that JSON and recreates/updates Group and Permission objects, linking by content type. Missing content types are skipped.
Notes: File handle in import_groups is opened without a context manager.

User Story: As a developer, I want a method that aggregates all permissions across all configured Django auth backends.
Expected Behavior: methods.all_permissions iterates backends and collects get_all_permissions results. models.User is the active user model patched with User.all_permissions = methods.User.all_permissions.

User Story: As a developer, I want a base test class with DRF client, user/permission helpers, and YAML/JSON/GraphQL utilities.
Expected Behavior: RestTestCase (TransactionTestCase) provides create_client, create_user, requester, assertResponse, Y/to_yaml/to_json debug helpers, and graphql(path, **params) that loads .graphql files and runs via apibase.utils.query.

User Story: As a developer, I want factory classes with fixture loading, GraphQL fixture execution, and sequence reset helpers.
Expected Behavior: FixtureMixin provides fuzzychoice, get_app_label, fixture/load_fixture, graphql_query, gql(id), from_json, qs, contenttype, from_fixture, create_from_list, and reset_sequence_by_id.

User Story: As a user or contributor, I want a documentation site with guides, tutorials, reference, and architecture docs.
Expected Behavior: docs/ contains MkDocs Material source. mkdocs.yml configures the site. .github/workflows/docs.yml builds/deploys to GitHub Pages with mike for versioning.

User Story: As a contributor, I want consistent linting and formatting rules configured in the project.
Expected Behavior: pyproject.toml configures Ruff with line-length 119, select rules E4/E7/E9/F/B/W/I/C4/UP, per-file ignores, and import sorting.

User Story: As a contributor, I want pytest to configure Django at module-import time so that apibase converter registration works during collection.
Expected Behavior: conftest.py configures Django settings (SQLite in-memory, contenttypes, auth, tests apps, MIGRATION_MODULES={'tests': None}) before apibase/__init__.py runs.
Notes: Harness fixed 2026-06-24: wired pytest-django via [tool.pytest.ini_options] DJANGO_SETTINGS_MODULE=tests.settings plus tests/settings.py. Root cause was pytest-django/pyyaml/drf-spectacular not synced into the devenv venv; uv sync --extra dev --extra schema resolves it. Suite now 130 passed (was 67p/7f).
